name: Wavelength: Jet Lag & Sleep
subtitle: Beat jet lag, sleep better

screenshots.caption: Land on local time: it tells you when to chase daylight and when to avoid it
screenshots.alt: Jet lag reading: a bright morning light that shifts your sleep toward the destination time zone

screenshots.caption: Your camera reads the light you are in and whether it is helping or hurting your sleep
screenshots.alt: Daytime reading: this light is strengthening your circadian rhythm

screenshots.caption: After sundown the whole app goes dark and confirms your room is dim enough to sleep
screenshots.alt: Night mode: well under the limit, good, keep it dim, with the screen faded to near-black

screenshots.caption: Holding a new schedule or night shift? It walks you through every step
screenshots.alt: Late-night reading: this light is shifting your sleep later to hold a night schedule

description:
Cross a few time zones and your body clock stays on home time for days: wide awake at 3 a.m., wrecked at noon. Wavelength fixes that. Your iPhone camera becomes a light meter for your body clock, and the app tells you exactly when to get daylight and when to avoid it, so you land closer to local time and stop losing the first days of every trip to fog.

The same thing that drags out jet lag, light reaching your eyes at the wrong hour, is also what keeps you up at night and foggy by day at home. Wavelength makes that light visible and tells you what to do about it.

Most circadian apps stop halfway. A light meter hands you a number and walks away. A coaching app builds you a plan but never sees the light you are actually in. Wavelength does both. It measures your real light and coaches your real schedule.

SEE YOUR LIGHT

Point your camera at the room. Wavelength gives you an instant color-coded reading and a clear verdict: at target, well under the limit, or too bright for this hour. No setup, no wearable, no accessories.

GET TOLD WHAT TO DO

A reading on its own changes nothing. Wavelength weighs your schedule, the time of day, and the direction you want to move, then tells you to get light or avoid light right now and when the next window opens.

JET LAG AND NEW SCHEDULES

Before and during a trip, Wavelength runs your itinerary against the destination time zone and tells you when to chase the sun and when to hide from it, so you arrive ready instead of fighting your body for a week. The same model handles a move to an earlier or later schedule at home.

FEEL BETTER AT HOME

Set your current bedtime and the bedtime you want, and Wavelength schedules the nudges that matter:
- When to start dimming before bed
- When bright daytime light will keep you sharp
- When light would work against your sleep

NIGHT MODE

After sundown the whole app fades to near-black so the screen stays out of your way, and it confirms your room is dim enough to sleep.

DESIGNED FOR

- Frequent travelers who want to beat jet lag and wake on local time
- Anyone who wants to fall asleep at the hour they want and feel sharp by day
- Shift workers holding a rhythm through nights: protect daytime sleep, stay alert on shift
- Night owls and early birds trying to move a clock that has drifted

BUILT ON PUBLISHED SCIENCE

Wavelength estimates melanopic light, the blue-rich light that sets your body clock, and judges it against the expert-consensus thresholds in Brown et al. (2022), PLOS Biology:
- Daytime: bright light for alertness
- Evening: low light before bed
- Sleep: near darkness

The timing model, how light at a given hour shifts your clock, follows Hannay et al. (2019), Journal of Biological Rhythms.

Research links better-aligned sleep to steadier mood and daytime focus. Wavelength supports sleep and circadian alignment. It does not diagnose, treat, or cure any medical condition.

WHAT YOU NEED

An iPhone with a camera. Nothing else. Camera measurement is approximate, not a laboratory spectrometer, but it is well-suited to the moment-to-moment comparisons that matter for your body clock.
note:
This is a preview, not the live App Store listing.
Screenshots are the exact 6.7" PNGs from the app build, shown bare as Apple displays them. The third frame is the app's night mode: the whole UI fades to near-black before bed.
